notes_md:
**cả** means **all / the whole / both**. It adds the idea of “including everything” or “not leaving anything out.”

It goes before a noun or noun phrase to emphasize totality: the whole thing, the whole group, the whole time.  

It’s stronger than just listing things — it stresses completeness.  
Very close to **tất cả**, but **cả** is shorter and often more flexible in sentences.

example.en: My whole family loves to eat pho.
example.es: Toda mi familia ama comer pho.
example.vi: Cả nhà tôi đều thích ăn phở.

definitions.vi: Toàn bộ; tất cả; toàn thể.

exampleSentencesTranslated: I buy all the bread and milk.
He reads all the books and newspapers.
He takes all the shirts and pants.
notes:
cả means all / the whole / both. It adds the idea of “including everything” or “not leaving anything out.”

It goes before a noun or noun phrase to emphasize totality: the whole thing, the whole group, the whole time.  

It’s stronger than just listing things — it stresses completeness.  
Very close to tất cả, but cả is shorter and often more flexible in sentences.
sentenceBreakdowns: Em (I) + mua (buy) + cả (all) + bánh (bread) + và (and) + sữa (milk)
Anh (he) + đọc (read) + cả (all) + sách (books) + và (and) + báo (newspapers)
Anh (he) + lấy (take) + cả (all) + áo (shirts) + và (and) + quần (pants)
exampleSentences: Em mua cả bánh và sữa.
Anh đọc cả sách và báo.
Anh lấy cả áo và quần.
